metadata.focus: flexibility, mobility, injury prevention for heavy/beginner individuals

facts.fact: Static stretching improves ROM via two mechanisms: increased stretch tolerance (neural) and reduced muscle-tendon stiffness (mechanical)
30-60 second static stretch holds produce meaningful ROM improvements; diminishing returns beyond 60 seconds per set
Chronic stretching requires 5+ sessions per week for 6+ weeks for permanent ROM improvements
Individuals with poor baseline flexibility show the greatest improvements from stretching programs

sources.source_name: PubMed - Konrad et al. 2025 (Sports Med Open)

facts.fact: Flexibility gains from stretching are lost within 4-8 weeks after cessation; ROM returns to baseline within ~30 days of stopping

sources.source_name: PubMed - Matsuo et al. 2025 (J Sports Sci Med)

facts.fact: Dynamic stretching for 5-10 min before exercise improves performance by 2-5%
Static stretching >60s before explosive activity REDUCES power output by 3-8%
Ballistic stretching produces similar acute gains to dynamic stretching but with higher injury risk
Post-exercise stretching reduces DOMS by only 1-4% out of 100 — statistically significant but clinically negligible

sources.source_name: PubMed - Jochum et al. 2025 (J Sport Health Sci)

facts.fact: Superimposed vibration during stretching increases flexibility gains by 5-10% compared to static stretching alone
Stretching before exercise does NOT significantly reduce injury risk — multiple large Cochrane reviews confirm this
Strength training reduces sports injury risk by 33-68% across multiple sports — the single most effective injury prevention intervention
At 130 kg, knees experience 3-4x body weight walking and 7-8x during stair climbing — joint-friendly exercise selection is critical
Spine is designed for stability, not mobility — back pain comes from insufficient core stability or excessive spinal flexion under load
Limited ankle dorsiflexion (>5cm on knee-to-wall test) causes compensations leading to knee pain, hip impingement, and lower back issues
Tight hip flexors tilt pelvis forward, compress lower back, and inhibit glutes — stretch 3x daily, 30-45s per side
McGill Big 3: modified curl-up, side plank, bird-dog — evidence-based gold standard for core stability and back injury prevention
Core functions to LIMIT excessive spinal motion; isometric exercises are superior to dynamic for spine health
Sit-ups place 3,300+ Newtons of compressive load on spinal discs per rep — not recommended especially for heavy individuals
Modified curl-up: lie on back, one leg straight one bent, hands under lower back, lift head/shoulders barely off ground, hold 10s, 3x6-8 reps
Bird-dog: on hands and knees, extend opposite arm/leg, hold 10s, 3x6-8 per side — trains anti-rotation stability
Complete rest is NEVER the fix for back pain — active rehabilitation is required
Walking 20-30+ min daily is therapeutic for the spine — gentle loading/unloading pumps discs with nutrients
Short-stop squat: press hands into thighs while bending forward to create core stiffness and force movement through hips
Knee pain in heavy individuals is often caused by weak glutes/hips, not the knee itself
Safe mobility at 130kg: seated hip stretches, wall ankle dorsiflexion, cat-cow, doorframe shoulder stretches, seated spinal twists
Mobility ≠ Flexibility: flexibility is passive ROM, mobility is active controlled ROM — you need both
10-minute daily mobility: 2 min each on 5 most restricted areas — consistency > duration
Best posture is the next posture — no single sitting position is optimal, change positions every 20-30 min
CARs (Controlled Articular Rotations): slow deliberate joint circles at max intentional range, 3-5 reps per joint daily
End-range isometrics: holding contraction at maximum accessible range builds strength in new ROM, 3-5x5-10s holds
PAILS/RAILS: isometric contractions in stretched positions to expand ROM, 30-45s holds per direction

sources.source_name: Functional Movement Systems (Gray Cook / Mike Boyle)

facts.fact: Joint-by-joint approach: Ankles(mobility)→Knees(stability)→Hips(mobility)→Lumbar(stability)→T-spine(mobility)→Shoulders(mobility)
Injuries occur when a mobility joint becomes stiff and the adjacent stability joint compensates with excessive movement
PNF stretching produces 10-15% greater acute ROM gains than static stretching
RAMP warm-up protocol: Raise→Activate→Mobilise→Potentiate, 10-15 min total
Dynamic warm-up 8-12 min improves performance 2-5%, larger effect in untrained individuals
Foam rolling 30-60s per muscle provides acute ROM and soreness reduction but no long-term tissue changes
Active recovery at <50% max effort for 20-30 min reduces DOMS faster than complete rest
Massage within 2h post-exercise reduces DOMS by 20-30%
Sitting >8h/day increases cardiovascular mortality 15-20% but 60-75 min daily exercise eliminates this risk
Standing desks optimal ratio: 2:1 sit:stand, not standing all day
Head weighs 5kg neutral but 25+kg at 60° forward tilt — why phone use causes neck pain
Modified yoga for heavy individuals improves flexibility, reduces stress, builds body awareness
Restorative yoga reduces cortisol and may aid weight management via parasympathetic activation
Chair yoga provides similar flexibility benefits for those who cannot comfortably use the floor
Yoga 2-3x/week for 8+ weeks produces 10-20% ROM improvement in sedentary adults
Water-based mobility eliminates joint loading — water supports 80% body weight in chest-deep water
Contrast water therapy: 1 min hot (38-40°C) / 1 min cold (12-15°C), repeat 3-4x accelerates recovery
Progressive overload at 5-10% weekly volume increases maximum is the best injury prevention for beginners
Core stability training reduces lower back pain recurrence by 30-40%
Flexibility improvements transfer poorly between joints — each muscle group must be targeted individually
